title: Minimizing Hallucination in Practice
concept: RAG, prompt constraints, and strict temperature settings are the primary defense against hallucination. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Red-Teaming & Safety Evals.'

exercises.prompt: What does 'hallucination' mean when we talk about AI behavior?

options.text: The AI generates convincing but completely fake information
The AI refuses to answer any questions
The AI processes images instead of text

exercises.explain: Hallucination is when an AI confidently presents incorrect or fabricated information as if it were true.

cards.title: Keeping AI Honest
cards.body: AI models love to please us, so much so that they will confidently make up fake facts (hallucinate) just to give an answer. How do we keep them honest and anchored to the truth in real-world apps?

cards.title: The Triple Defense
cards.body: To stop AI from hallucinating (making things up), we use a three-layer defense: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to give it real documents, prompt constraints to forbid guessing, and low temperature settings to keep its answers predictable.

cards.title: The Open-Book Exam
cards.body: Imagine you have an open-book history exam. If you try to answer from memory (general training), you might confuse dates. But if you have the textbook open right in front of you (RAG) and the teacher tells you to ONLY write down what is on page 42 (prompt constraints), you are much less likely to make things up!

cards.title: Fact-Checking in Action
cards.body: A medical AI is asked about a new drug. Instead of guessing, it uses RAG to search a database of approved medical papers, reads a constraint saying "If not found in the paper, say I don't know", and uses a temperature of 0.0 to stay completely factual.

cards.title: Is a Simple Warning Enough?
cards.body: Many believe that simply telling an AI 'don't lie' is enough. In reality, large models will still hallucinate unless you actively feed them verified documents (RAG) and lock down their creativity settings.

cards.title: Creative vs. Factual Mode
cards.body: See how the settings change the AI's behavior when asked a factual question.

left.text: Answers from memory, high creativity, high risk of hallucinating fake details.

right.text: Reads verified documents, zero creativity, strictly matches the facts.

exercises.prompt: Which technique provides the AI with external, verified documents to read before answering?
exercises.explain: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) feeds the AI real-time, verified documents so it doesn't have to guess from memory.

exercises.prompt: To minimize hallucinations, you should set the AI's temperature setting as high as possible.
exercises.explain: Higher temperatures increase randomness and creativity, which actually increases the risk of hallucination. We want low temperature for facts.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the defense strategy.
exercises.sentence: To make an AI's responses highly predictable and factual, we should lower its temperature.
exercises.explain: Setting the temperature close to 0 makes the AI highly predictable and focused on facts rather than creative guesses.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a system prompt constraint designed to stop hallucinations?

options.text: 'If the answer is not in the provided text, reply with: I do not know.'

exercises.explain: System prompt constraints explicitly tell the AI what it is allowed to do, such as admitting when it doesn't know the answer.

exercises.prompt: You are building a customer service bot that must never lie about product prices. What is the best strategy to prevent hallucinations?

options.text: Increase the temperature to maximum to make the AI creative
Rely entirely on the model's pre-trained memory without extra context
Combine RAG, strict system prompt constraints, and a low temperature
Remove all instructions and let the AI guess the facts

exercises.explain: RAG, strict constraints, and low temperatures work together to anchor the AI to real facts, preventing it from making things up. Next up: Red-Teaming & Safety Evals.
